name: Experimental Psychology
description: An introduction to the scientific methods, research designs, statistical tools, and ethical standards used to conduct and evaluate psychological research.

modules.title: Science & the Scientific Method
modules.objectives: Describe the goals and characteristics of science, explain the steps of the scientific method, and distinguish science from pseudoscience.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Research Methods in Psychology
modules.assignment: Identify a popular claim in the media (e.g., a wellness trend). Apply the steps of the scientific method to evaluate it: state a testable hypothesis, describe how you could test it, and identify what evidence would support or refute it.

modules.title: Research Ethics
modules.objectives: Explain the key principles of ethical research (informed consent, deception, debriefing, confidentiality), and evaluate landmark ethical violations in psychology.
modules.assignment: Analyze the Tuskegee Syphilis Study or the Stanford Prison Experiment. Identify three specific ethical violations using APA guidelines and explain what safeguards modern IRBs would require for a similar study today.

modules.title: Observational Methods
modules.objectives: Distinguish between naturalistic and structured observation, describe participant vs. non-participant observation, and evaluate the strengths and limitations of observational research.
modules.assignment: Conduct a 20-minute naturalistic observation in a public setting (e.g., a cafeteria or library). Record your observations using a structured coding sheet, then evaluate what the observer effect might have influenced.

modules.title: Survey & Self-Report Methods
modules.objectives: Explain the principles of effective survey design, identify common response biases, and compare open-ended and closed-ended question formats.
modules.assignment: Design a 10-question survey to study student stress. Include both Likert-scale and open-ended items. Identify two potential sources of bias in your survey and explain how you would minimize them.

modules.title: Correlational Research
modules.objectives: Explain what a correlation coefficient measures, interpret scatter plots, and explain why correlation does not imply causation.
modules.assignment: Find a real published correlation in psychology (e.g., screen time and depression). Describe the correlation coefficient, evaluate its strength, and generate two plausible third-variable explanations for the relationship.

modules.title: Experimental Design
modules.objectives: Identify the components of a true experiment, distinguish between between-subjects and within-subjects designs, and explain the importance of control groups.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Between vs. Within Subjects Designs
modules.assignment: Design a complete experiment testing whether study breaks improve test performance. Specify the IV, DV, control group, participant assignment method, and how you would control for at least two potential confounds.

modules.title: Variables & Control
modules.objectives: Operationally define independent, dependent, and confounding variables, explain experimental control techniques, and recognize sources of bias.
modules.assignment: Read a summary of a published psychology experiment. Identify the independent variable, dependent variable, and two potential confounding variables. Suggest one specific control procedure for each confound you identify.

modules.title: Sampling Methods
modules.objectives: Compare probability and non-probability sampling techniques, explain sampling error, and evaluate how sample characteristics affect generalizability.
modules.assignment: A researcher wants to study social media use among US high school students. Describe how you would use stratified random sampling to create a representative sample, and explain why simple random sampling might not be sufficient.

modules.title: Data Collection
modules.objectives: Distinguish qualitative from quantitative data, explain reliability and validity of measures, and describe standardized testing and psychophysiological recording methods.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Reliability and Validity in Research
modules.assignment: Develop an operational definition for 'academic stress' and create two ways to measure it — one self-report and one behavioral measure. Evaluate the reliability and validity of each measurement approach.

modules.title: Descriptive Statistics
modules.objectives: Calculate and interpret measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) and variability (range, standard deviation), and describe distributions using graphs.
modules.assignment: Collect the daily study hours of 15 classmates for one week. Calculate the mean, median, mode, and standard deviation for the dataset. Create a frequency histogram and describe the shape of the distribution.

modules.title: Inferential Statistics
modules.objectives: Explain hypothesis testing, define p-values and statistical significance, distinguish between Type I and Type II errors, and interpret basic t-test results.
modules.assignment: Given a dataset comparing test scores of students who slept 8 hours vs. 6 hours, write a null and alternative hypothesis, identify whether the p-value of 0.03 is statistically significant at α = 0.05, and interpret the result in plain language.

modules.title: Interpreting Results
modules.objectives: Explain statistical vs. practical significance, describe effect size, and identify limitations and alternative explanations when interpreting research findings.
modules.assignment: Read an abstract from a published psychology study. Identify the main finding, evaluate whether statistical significance implies practical significance, and propose one alternative explanation for the results.

modules.title: Writing Research Reports
modules.objectives: Describe the APA format for psychology research reports, explain the purpose of each section (Abstract, Introduction, Method, Results, Discussion), and practice scholarly citation.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Write a Psychology Lab Report (APA)
modules.assignment: Write a complete APA-formatted Methods section for an imaginary study on how background noise affects memory. Include Participants, Materials, and Procedure subsections with all required detail for replication.

modules.title: Critical Evaluation of Studies
modules.objectives: Apply criteria for evaluating research quality (internal and external validity, replication, peer review), recognize flawed claims, and understand the replication crisis.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Replication Crisis in Psychology
modules.assignment: Critically evaluate a media report of a psychological study. Identify the original research it describes, assess threats to internal and external validity, and explain how you would communicate the study's limitations to a general audience.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT a goal of psychological science?
quizQuestions.answer: Entertainment
quizQuestions.explanation: The four goals of psychology are description, explanation, prediction, and control/application — not entertainment.

quizQuestions.question: A testable prediction derived from a theory is called a:
quizQuestions.answer: Hypothesis
quizQuestions.explanation: A hypothesis is a specific, testable prediction about the relationship between variables derived from theory.

quizQuestions.question: Pseudoscience differs from science primarily because it:
options: Resists disconfirmation and lacks peer review
quizQuestions.answer: Resists disconfirmation and lacks peer review
quizQuestions.explanation: Pseudoscience makes unfalsifiable claims and does not subject its findings to rigorous peer review or replication.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of falsifiability, central to science, means a claim must be:
options: Capable of being shown false by evidence
quizQuestions.answer: Capable of being shown false by evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: Falsifiability (Popper) requires that a scientific claim can be contradicted by observable evidence — unfalsifiable claims are not scientific.

quizQuestions.question: Peer review in science serves primarily to:
options: Ensure research is evaluated by experts before publication
Allow the public to comment on studies
quizQuestions.answer: Ensure research is evaluated by experts before publication
quizQuestions.explanation: Peer review subjects research to scrutiny by independent experts, helping to catch errors and improve quality before publication.

quizQuestions.question: Informed consent requires that participants:
options: Are fully deceived about the study's purpose
Are told enough about the study to make a voluntary decision to participate
Cannot withdraw from a study once begun
quizQuestions.answer: Are told enough about the study to make a voluntary decision to participate
quizQuestions.explanation: Informed consent is an ethical requirement that participants receive sufficient information about the study to decide whether to participate voluntarily.

quizQuestions.question: Debriefing in research involves:
options: Explaining the true purpose of the study after it ends
Obtaining consent before the study begins
quizQuestions.answer: Explaining the true purpose of the study after it ends
quizQuestions.explanation: Debriefing informs participants about any deception used and the full purpose of the study, allowing them to make sense of their experience.

quizQuestions.question: The Belmont Report established which three core principles of research ethics?
quizQuestions.answer: Respect, justice, beneficence
quizQuestions.explanation: The Belmont Report (1979) identified three principles: respect for persons (autonomy), beneficence (do good/avoid harm), and justice (fair distribution of benefits and risks).

quizQuestions.question: Using deception in research is ethically justified only when:
options: The study is funded by a major institution
There is no other way to study the phenomenon and participants are debriefed
The study involves minimal risk with no debriefing needed
Participants cannot determine the real purpose during the study
quizQuestions.answer: There is no other way to study the phenomenon and participants are debriefed
quizQuestions.explanation: Deception is permissible only if it is scientifically necessary, risk is minimal, and participants receive a complete debriefing afterward.

quizQuestions.question: IRBs (Institutional Review Boards) evaluate research proposals primarily on the basis of:
options: Protection of participants from harm and ethical standards
quizQuestions.answer: Protection of participants from harm and ethical standards
quizQuestions.explanation: IRBs review research to ensure ethical treatment of participants, weighing risks against benefits and verifying ethical procedures.

quizQuestions.question: Naturalistic observation requires researchers to:
options: Observe behavior without interfering in natural settings
quizQuestions.answer: Observe behavior without interfering in natural settings
quizQuestions.explanation: Naturalistic observation involves watching and recording behavior as it occurs naturally, without intervention.

quizQuestions.question: The observer effect (Hawthorne effect) refers to:
options: Behavior changing because participants know they are being observed
The difficulty of remaining objective while observing
quizQuestions.answer: Behavior changing because participants know they are being observed
quizQuestions.explanation: The Hawthorne effect occurs when awareness of being observed changes participants' behavior, threatening the naturalness of the observation.

quizQuestions.question: Inter-rater reliability in observational research refers to:
options: Whether the same observer gives consistent ratings over time
Agreement between two or more independent observers
Whether the measure captures what it intends to measure
The consistency of the observation coding scheme across studies
quizQuestions.answer: Agreement between two or more independent observers
quizQuestions.explanation: Inter-rater reliability measures the degree of agreement between independent observers using the same coding system.

quizQuestions.question: A key limitation of naturalistic observation is:
options: It cannot describe behavior as it naturally occurs
It does not allow establishment of cause-and-effect relationships
It cannot be used for ethical research
quizQuestions.answer: It does not allow establishment of cause-and-effect relationships
quizQuestions.explanation: Because no variables are manipulated, naturalistic observation cannot establish causation — only describe naturally occurring behavior.

quizQuestions.question: A structured observation differs from naturalistic observation in that:
options: Specific behaviors are coded using a predetermined system
It avoids any influence on participants
It uses a laboratory setting only
quizQuestions.answer: Specific behaviors are coded using a predetermined system
quizQuestions.explanation: Structured observation uses a predefined coding scheme to systematically record specific target behaviors.

quizQuestions.question: A Likert scale typically measures:
options: Degree of agreement or disagreement on a numbered scale
quizQuestions.answer: Degree of agreement or disagreement on a numbered scale
quizQuestions.explanation: Likert scales ask respondents to rate their agreement from strongly disagree to strongly agree, providing ordered response options.

quizQuestions.question: Social desirability bias in surveys refers to respondents:
options: Giving answers they believe are socially acceptable rather than truthful
quizQuestions.answer: Giving answers they believe are socially acceptable rather than truthful
quizQuestions.explanation: Social desirability bias occurs when respondents give answers that make them look good rather than answering honestly.

quizQuestions.question: A leading question in survey research is problematic because it:
options: Suggests the desired answer within the question
quizQuestions.answer: Suggests the desired answer within the question
quizQuestions.explanation: Leading questions bias responses by hinting at a desired or expected answer, compromising the validity of survey data.

quizQuestions.question: Open-ended survey questions are advantageous because they:
options: Capture a range of unexpected responses in participants' own words
quizQuestions.answer: Capture a range of unexpected responses in participants' own words
quizQuestions.explanation: Open-ended questions allow respondents to answer freely, potentially revealing unexpected themes not anticipated by the researcher.

quizQuestions.question: Random sampling in surveys helps ensure:
options: All participants answer the same questions
No bias exists in question wording
quizQuestions.answer: The sample represents the population
quizQuestions.explanation: Random sampling gives every member of the population an equal chance of selection, producing a representative sample.

quizQuestions.question: A correlation coefficient of +0.90 indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: A strong positive relationship
quizQuestions.explanation: Correlation coefficients close to +1.0 indicate a strong positive (direct) relationship between two variables.

quizQuestions.question: A third variable problem in correlational research means:
options: A third variable directly causes both measured variables
Three variables must always be measured simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: A third variable directly causes both measured variables
quizQuestions.explanation: The third-variable problem occurs when an unmeasured variable explains the observed correlation between two measured variables.

quizQuestions.question: A scatter plot showing points clustering close to a downward-sloping line indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: A strong negative correlation
quizQuestions.explanation: A tight cluster of points along a downward slope indicates a strong negative (inverse) relationship between the two variables.

quizQuestions.question: Directionality is a problem in correlational research because:
options: We cannot determine which variable causes the other
We cannot measure both variables simultaneously
Sample size affects the correlation coefficient
quizQuestions.answer: We cannot determine which variable causes the other
quizQuestions.explanation: Even if two variables are correlated, the direction of causation (A→B or B→A) cannot be determined from correlational data alone.

quizQuestions.question: Illusory correlation refers to:
options: A correlation that weakens over time
Perceiving a relationship between variables that does not actually exist
A correlation that is statistically significant but practically trivial
A correlation caused by a third variable
quizQuestions.answer: Perceiving a relationship between variables that does not actually exist
quizQuestions.explanation: Illusory correlation is the tendency to perceive a relationship between two variables when none actually exists.

quizQuestions.question: Random assignment in experiments is used to:
options: Select a representative sample from the population
Ensure all participants experience all conditions equally
Distribute individual differences evenly across experimental groups
Increase the size of the sample
quizQuestions.answer: Distribute individual differences evenly across experimental groups
quizQuestions.explanation: Random assignment distributes participant characteristics randomly across conditions, making groups equivalent before the IV is manipulated.

quizQuestions.question: In a within-subjects (repeated measures) design, each participant:
options: Is assigned to only one condition
Experiences all conditions of the experiment
Is matched with a participant in another condition
Is compared only to the control group
quizQuestions.answer: Experiences all conditions of the experiment
quizQuestions.explanation: In a within-subjects design, the same participants are tested in all conditions, reducing individual-difference confounds.

quizQuestions.question: A control group in an experiment serves as:
options: The group receiving the highest dose of the treatment
A baseline for comparison with the experimental group
The group assigned randomly to conditions
A replication of the experimental group
quizQuestions.answer: A baseline for comparison with the experimental group
quizQuestions.explanation: The control group receives no treatment (or a placebo) and provides a baseline against which to measure the effect of the independent variable.

quizQuestions.question: A double-blind experiment controls for:
options: Both participant expectations and experimenter bias
quizQuestions.answer: Both participant expectations and experimenter bias
quizQuestions.explanation: In a double-blind design, neither participants nor researchers know who received the treatment, eliminating expectation and experimenter bias.

quizQuestions.question: A factorial design involves:
options: Testing one IV at multiple levels
Studying the effects of two or more IVs simultaneously
Using only one group of participants
quizQuestions.answer: Studying the effects of two or more IVs simultaneously
quizQuestions.explanation: A factorial design examines multiple independent variables and their interactions within a single study.

quizQuestions.question: An operational definition specifies:
options: The theoretical meaning of a construct
How a variable will be measured or manipulated in a specific study
The statistical analysis to be used
The ethical justification for the study
quizQuestions.answer: How a variable will be measured or manipulated in a specific study
quizQuestions.explanation: Operational definitions translate abstract constructs into concrete, measurable procedures used in a specific study.

quizQuestions.question: Experimenter bias occurs when:
options: The researcher's expectations influence the outcome of the study
quizQuestions.answer: The researcher's expectations influence the outcome of the study
quizQuestions.explanation: Experimenter bias is the unintentional influence of a researcher's expectations on how they collect, interpret, or report data.

quizQuestions.question: A confounding variable is one that:
options: Is deliberately manipulated by the researcher
Co-varies with the IV and provides an alternative explanation for results
Is the primary outcome measured in the study
quizQuestions.answer: Co-varies with the IV and provides an alternative explanation for results
quizQuestions.explanation: A confound is an uncontrolled variable that changes along with the IV, making it impossible to determine which variable caused the outcome.

quizQuestions.question: Demand characteristics occur when participants:
options: Try to behave as they think the experimenter wants them to
Drop out of the study early
quizQuestions.answer: Try to behave as they think the experimenter wants them to
quizQuestions.explanation: Demand characteristics are cues in the experimental setting that hint at the expected behavior, potentially biasing participants' responses.

quizQuestions.question: Counterbalancing in within-subjects designs is used to control for:
quizQuestions.answer: Order effects (practice and fatigue effects)
quizQuestions.explanation: Counterbalancing varies the order in which conditions are experienced across participants, distributing order effects equally.

quizQuestions.question: A simple random sample is one in which:
options: Every member of the population has an equal chance of selection
The sample is stratified by demographic groups
Participants are selected based on convenience
The researcher selects 'typical' members of the population
quizQuestions.answer: Every member of the population has an equal chance of selection
quizQuestions.explanation: In simple random sampling, all members of the population have an equal and independent probability of being selected.

quizQuestions.question: Stratified random sampling divides the population into subgroups and then:
options: Selects all members of one subgroup
Randomly samples from each subgroup in proportion to its size
quizQuestions.answer: Randomly samples from each subgroup in proportion to its size
quizQuestions.explanation: Stratified sampling ensures proportional representation of key subgroups by randomly sampling within each stratum.

quizQuestions.question: A convenience sample is a limitation because it:
options: Is too large for statistical analysis
Is unlikely to be representative of the population
Cannot be used in correlational research
quizQuestions.answer: Is unlikely to be representative of the population
quizQuestions.explanation: Convenience samples consist of participants who are easily available, which can introduce systematic bias and limit generalizability.

quizQuestions.question: Sampling error refers to:
options: The difference between sample statistics and true population parameters due to chance
quizQuestions.answer: The difference between sample statistics and true population parameters due to chance
quizQuestions.explanation: Sampling error is the natural variability between a sample statistic and the true population parameter caused by chance selection.

quizQuestions.question: The WEIRD problem in psychology research refers to the over-representation of:
options: Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic populations
quizQuestions.answer: Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic populations
quizQuestions.explanation: The WEIRD critique notes that most psychology research uses samples from Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic societies, limiting global generalizability.

quizQuestions.question: Construct validity refers to whether a measure:
options: Accurately captures the theoretical construct it is intended to measure
Produces results consistent with other established measures
Discriminates between groups known to differ on the construct
quizQuestions.answer: Accurately captures the theoretical construct it is intended to measure
quizQuestions.explanation: Construct validity assesses whether a test truly measures the psychological construct it claims to measure.

quizQuestions.question: Test-retest reliability is assessed by:
options: Comparing two different tests measuring the same construct
Administering the same test to the same group twice and correlating scores
Having two raters score the same responses
Splitting a test in half and correlating the halves
quizQuestions.answer: Administering the same test to the same group twice and correlating scores
quizQuestions.explanation: Test-retest reliability measures consistency over time by administering the same instrument twice and computing the correlation.

quizQuestions.question: A measure is valid but not reliable — is this possible?
quizQuestions.answer: No, validity requires reliability
quizQuestions.explanation: A measure cannot be valid (accurately measuring what it intends) if it is unreliable (inconsistent), because inconsistent results cannot accurately represent a true construct.

quizQuestions.question: Content validity refers to whether a test:
options: Adequately covers all aspects of the construct being measured
Correlates with other accepted measures of the construct
quizQuestions.answer: Adequately covers all aspects of the construct being measured
quizQuestions.explanation: Content validity assesses whether the items of a test representatively sample the full domain of the construct being measured.

quizQuestions.question: Psychophysiological measures (e.g., heart rate, skin conductance) are used in psychology research because they:
options: Are easier to collect than self-report data
Provide objective indicators of internal states
Replace the need for behavioral observation
Eliminate all sources of measurement error
quizQuestions.answer: Provide objective indicators of internal states
quizQuestions.explanation: Psychophysiological measures provide objective, continuous recordings of physiological responses associated with psychological states.

quizQuestions.question: In a normal distribution, the mean, median, and mode are:
quizQuestions.answer: The same value
quizQuestions.explanation: In a perfectly normal (bell-shaped) distribution, the mean, median, and mode are equal and located at the center of the distribution.

quizQuestions.question: Standard deviation measures:
options: The highest value in a dataset
The average distance of scores from the mean
quizQuestions.answer: The average distance of scores from the mean
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard deviation quantifies the average spread of scores around the mean — larger SD indicates more variability.

quizQuestions.question: A positively skewed distribution has:
options: A tail extending to the left
More scores at the high end
A tail extending to the right
quizQuestions.answer: A tail extending to the right
quizQuestions.explanation: In a positively skewed distribution, most scores cluster at the lower end with a long tail extending toward higher values.

quizQuestions.question: The median is preferred over the mean when data are:
quizQuestions.answer: Skewed or contain extreme outliers
quizQuestions.explanation: The median is resistant to outliers and is a better measure of central tendency when distributions are skewed.

quizQuestions.question: A frequency histogram displays:
options: The distribution of scores across intervals
quizQuestions.answer: The distribution of scores across intervals
quizQuestions.explanation: A frequency histogram uses bars to show how often scores fall within each interval, revealing the distribution's shape.

quizQuestions.question: The null hypothesis in a study states that:
options: There is a significant difference between groups
Any observed difference is due to chance (no real effect exists)
The independent variable will affect the dependent variable
The sample is representative of the population
quizQuestions.answer: Any observed difference is due to chance (no real effect exists)
quizQuestions.explanation: The null hypothesis assumes no effect or no relationship — observed differences are attributed to sampling variability.

quizQuestions.question: A p-value of 0.03 at α = 0.05 means:
options: There is a 3% chance the hypothesis is true
The result is statistically significant; we reject the null hypothesis
The result is not statistically significant
quizQuestions.answer: The result is statistically significant; we reject the null hypothesis
quizQuestions.explanation: Since p = 0.03 < α = 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis; the result is unlikely to be due to chance.

quizQuestions.question: A Type I error in hypothesis testing means:
options: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
Rejecting a true null hypothesis (false positive)
quizQuestions.answer: Rejecting a true null hypothesis (false positive)
quizQuestions.explanation: A Type I error is a false positive — concluding there is an effect when none actually exists; its probability equals α.

quizQuestions.question: Statistical power refers to the probability of:
options: Correctly detecting a true effect (rejecting a false null hypothesis)
Correctly retaining a true null hypothesis
quizQuestions.answer: Correctly detecting a true effect (rejecting a false null hypothesis)
quizQuestions.explanation: Statistical power is the probability that a study will correctly reject a false null hypothesis — detecting a real effect if one exists.

quizQuestions.question: Increasing sample size generally:
options: Increases statistical power and precision of estimates
Has no effect on significance testing
quizQuestions.answer: Increases statistical power and precision of estimates
quizQuestions.explanation: Larger samples reduce standard error, increase statistical power, and produce more precise estimates of population parameters.

quizQuestions.question: Effect size measures:
options: Whether a result is statistically significant
The practical magnitude of an effect independent of sample size
The probability of the null hypothesis being true
The direction of the relationship between variables
quizQuestions.answer: The practical magnitude of an effect independent of sample size
quizQuestions.explanation: Effect size (e.g., Cohen's d) quantifies the strength of an effect regardless of sample size, indicating practical significance.

quizQuestions.question: A statistically significant result with a very small effect size is best interpreted as:
options: Statistically meaningful but possibly practically trivial
Strong evidence for a major psychological phenomenon
quizQuestions.answer: Statistically meaningful but possibly practically trivial
quizQuestions.explanation: Large samples can make tiny effects statistically significant even when they have no practical importance — effect size reveals whether the effect matters in the real world.

quizQuestions.question: External validity refers to how well study findings:
options: Reflect the true causal effect within the study
Generalize to other people, settings, and times
quizQuestions.answer: Generalize to other people, settings, and times
quizQuestions.explanation: External validity (generalizability) is the degree to which study findings apply beyond the specific sample, setting, and time of the original research.

quizQuestions.question: Internal validity is threatened by:
options: Confounding variables that provide alternative explanations for the results
Low test-retest reliability of the DV measure
quizQuestions.answer: Confounding variables that provide alternative explanations for the results
quizQuestions.explanation: Internal validity is the degree to which the IV (and not something else) caused the change in the DV; confounds undermine this.

quizQuestions.question: Confidence intervals provide information about:
options: Whether the null hypothesis is true
The range within which the true population parameter likely falls
The probability of a Type I error only
Whether effect size is large or small
quizQuestions.answer: The range within which the true population parameter likely falls
quizQuestions.explanation: A 95% confidence interval indicates that if we repeated the study many times, 95% of the intervals constructed would contain the true population parameter.

quizQuestions.question: The APA-style research report sections appear in which order?
quizQuestions.answer: Abstract, Introduction, Method, Results, Discussion
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard APA reports follow this order: Title page, Abstract, Introduction, Method, Results, Discussion, References.

quizQuestions.question: The Abstract of a research report should:
options: Provide a detailed review of the literature
Briefly summarize the study's purpose, method, results, and conclusions
Describe the statistical analyses in detail
quizQuestions.answer: Briefly summarize the study's purpose, method, results, and conclusions
quizQuestions.explanation: The Abstract is a concise summary (usually 150–250 words) covering all major aspects of the study.

quizQuestions.question: The Method section of a report must be detailed enough to:
options: Explain why the study was conducted
Allow another researcher to replicate the study
quizQuestions.answer: Allow another researcher to replicate the study
quizQuestions.explanation: The Method section must provide sufficient detail about participants, materials, and procedures for exact replication.

quizQuestions.question: In APA style, an in-text citation for a work by Smith and Jones (2020) would appear as:
quizQuestions.answer: (Smith & Jones, 2020)
quizQuestions.explanation: APA in-text citations use parentheses with the author surname(s) and year, using '&' for multiple authors within parentheses.

quizQuestions.question: The Discussion section of a report should:
options: Interpret results, relate them to prior research, and acknowledge limitations
Describe the study's method in detail
quizQuestions.answer: Interpret results, relate them to prior research, and acknowledge limitations
quizQuestions.explanation: The Discussion interprets the findings in the context of existing theory and research, addresses limitations, and suggests future directions.

quizQuestions.question: The replication crisis in psychology refers to:
options: Many published findings failing to replicate in independent studies
Journal editors refusing to publish replications
Statistical methods being too complex to repeat
quizQuestions.answer: Many published findings failing to replicate in independent studies
quizQuestions.explanation: The replication crisis emerged when large-scale replication projects found that many classic psychology findings could not be reliably reproduced.

quizQuestions.question: Publication bias refers to the tendency for:
options: Significant results to be published more than non-significant results
Journals to prefer qualitative over quantitative studies
Replications to be published more than original studies
Negative findings to be published more often
quizQuestions.answer: Significant results to be published more than non-significant results
quizQuestions.explanation: Publication bias skews the scientific literature toward positive findings because null results are less likely to be submitted or accepted for publication.

quizQuestions.question: HARKing (Hypothesizing After Results are Known) is problematic because it:
options: Inflates false positive rates by treating post-hoc observations as predictions
quizQuestions.answer: Inflates false positive rates by treating post-hoc observations as predictions
quizQuestions.explanation: HARKing misrepresents exploratory findings as confirmatory predictions, inflating the apparent significance of results.

quizQuestions.question: Pre-registration of a study helps address:
options: HARKing and publication bias by specifying hypotheses and analyses before data collection
quizQuestions.answer: HARKing and publication bias by specifying hypotheses and analyses before data collection
quizQuestions.explanation: Pre-registration requires researchers to publicly document their hypotheses and analysis plans before collecting data, reducing flexibility and researcher degrees of freedom.

quizQuestions.question: A conceptual replication differs from a direct replication in that it:
options: Uses identical procedures to the original study
Tests the same hypothesis with different operationalizations or samples
Is conducted by the original research team
quizQuestions.answer: Tests the same hypothesis with different operationalizations or samples
quizQuestions.explanation: Conceptual replications test the theoretical claim using different methods, extending generalizability beyond the original procedure.

questions.question: Falsifiability means a hypothesis:
options: Can potentially be shown to be false by evidence
questions.answer: Can potentially be shown to be false by evidence
questions.explanation: Popper's criterion of falsifiability holds that scientific claims must be capable of being contradicted by observable evidence.

questions.question: Debriefing is ethically required after research involving:
questions.answer: Deception or potentially distressing procedures
questions.explanation: Debriefing is especially critical when deception or distress was involved, ensuring participants fully understand what occurred and why.

questions.question: The Hawthorne effect describes how participants:
options: Change behavior when they know they are being observed
questions.answer: Change behavior when they know they are being observed
questions.explanation: The Hawthorne effect is the alteration of behavior that occurs because individuals know they are being studied.

questions.question: Social desirability bias in surveys occurs when respondents:
options: Answer in ways they believe are socially acceptable rather than truthfully
questions.answer: Answer in ways they believe are socially acceptable rather than truthfully
questions.explanation: Social desirability bias leads participants to present themselves favorably rather than honestly.

questions.question: A scatter plot shows a tight cluster of points on a line sloping upward — this indicates:
questions.answer: A strong positive correlation
questions.explanation: A tight upward-sloping scatter indicates a strong positive relationship between the two variables.

questions.question: Random assignment ensures that:
options: The sample is representative of the population
Individual differences are evenly distributed across conditions
All participants receive the same treatment
Results will replicate in future studies
questions.answer: Individual differences are evenly distributed across conditions
questions.explanation: Random assignment makes experimental groups equivalent at the start, allowing the IV to be the explanation for any outcome differences.

questions.question: An operational definition translates an abstract variable into:
questions.answer: A specific, measurable procedure
questions.explanation: Operational definitions specify exactly how a variable will be measured or manipulated in a particular study.

questions.question: Stratified random sampling is used when researchers want to:
options: Ensure proportional representation of key subgroups
questions.answer: Ensure proportional representation of key subgroups
questions.explanation: Stratified sampling divides the population into subgroups and randomly samples from each, ensuring representative coverage.

questions.question: Construct validity refers to whether a test:
options: Truly measures the psychological construct it intends to measure
questions.answer: Truly measures the psychological construct it intends to measure
questions.explanation: Construct validity evaluates whether the measurement instrument accurately taps the theoretical construct it is designed to assess.

questions.question: When data are strongly skewed, the best measure of central tendency is:
questions.answer: Median
questions.explanation: The median is resistant to outliers and is more appropriate than the mean for skewed distributions.

questions.question: In a double-blind experiment, who does NOT know which participants received the treatment?
questions.answer: Both participants and the experimenter
questions.explanation: In a double-blind design, both participants and the people administering the study are unaware of condition assignment, controlling for multiple sources of bias.

questions.question: Inter-rater reliability is highest when two observers:
options: Come from the same research team
Agree closely on their independent ratings of the same event
questions.answer: Agree closely on their independent ratings of the same event
questions.explanation: Inter-rater reliability reflects the consistency of ratings between independent observers — higher agreement means higher reliability.

questions.question: The directionality problem in correlational research means:
options: We cannot determine which variable caused the other
Three variables must always be measured
questions.answer: We cannot determine which variable caused the other
questions.explanation: Correlational data show that two variables are related but cannot establish which one is the cause and which is the effect.

questions.question: Counterbalancing in a within-subjects design controls for:
options: Order effects such as practice and fatigue
questions.answer: Order effects such as practice and fatigue
questions.explanation: Counterbalancing systematically varies the order of conditions across participants so that order effects are distributed evenly.

questions.question: The null hypothesis assumes:
options: A meaningful effect exists in the population
Any observed difference is due to chance
The study's hypothesis will be supported
questions.answer: Any observed difference is due to chance
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis is the default assumption of no effect — differences observed are attributed to random variation.

questions.question: Effect size (Cohen's d) tells researchers:
options: Whether a result is statistically significant
The practical magnitude of an effect
The probability of a Type I error
The size of the sample needed
questions.answer: The practical magnitude of an effect
questions.explanation: Cohen's d quantifies the standardized difference between groups, indicating how large the effect is in practical terms.

questions.question: External validity is best improved by:
options: Increasing sample size of the original study
Using diverse samples and varied settings across studies
questions.answer: Using diverse samples and varied settings across studies
questions.explanation: External validity increases when findings hold across different participants, settings, and time periods.

questions.question: The APA Methods section is written with enough detail to:
options: Explain why the study is important
Allow exact replication of the study
questions.answer: Allow exact replication of the study
questions.explanation: The Methods section must provide sufficient procedural detail for independent researchers to replicate the study exactly.

questions.question: The replication crisis refers to:
options: Studies being too expensive to repeat
Many classic psychology findings failing to replicate
Statistical methods being inconsistent across labs
questions.answer: Many classic psychology findings failing to replicate
questions.explanation: Large replication projects found that a substantial proportion of published psychology findings could not be reproduced.

questions.question: A Type II error (false negative) means:
options: Failing to detect a real effect that exists
questions.answer: Failing to detect a real effect that exists
questions.explanation: A Type II error is accepting the null hypothesis when it is actually false — failing to detect a true effect.

questions.question: Publication bias leads to a scientific literature that:
options: Is perfectly representative of all research conducted
Favors qualitative studies over quantitative ones
questions.answer: Overrepresents statistically significant positive results
questions.explanation: Because positive findings are more likely to be published, the published literature overestimates effect sizes and the reliability of findings.

questions.question: Pre-registration of a study requires researchers to:
options: Specify hypotheses and analysis plans before data collection
Obtain IRB approval after data collection
questions.answer: Specify hypotheses and analysis plans before data collection
questions.explanation: Pre-registration transparently commits researchers to their hypotheses and analytic approaches before seeing the data.

questions.question: A 95% confidence interval that does NOT include zero suggests:
options: The result is not statistically significant
The null hypothesis cannot be evaluated
The effect is likely statistically significant
questions.answer: The effect is likely statistically significant
questions.explanation: If a 95% CI for a difference or regression coefficient excludes zero, the effect is statistically significant at α = 0.05.

questions.question: HARKing undermines scientific integrity because it:
options: Presents post-hoc findings as if they were a priori predictions
Reduces the number of analyses run
questions.answer: Presents post-hoc findings as if they were a priori predictions
questions.explanation: HARKing converts data-driven exploratory patterns into falsely confirmatory hypotheses, inflating false-positive rates.

questions.question: A conceptual replication tests whether:
options: The original study's procedure can be run exactly as before
The underlying theoretical claim holds using different methods or samples
Statistical analyses from the original study are correct
The original researchers' conclusions were accurate
questions.answer: The underlying theoretical claim holds using different methods or samples
questions.explanation: Conceptual replications assess the generalizability of a theoretical finding using different operationalizations or populations.

questions.question: Which sampling method is most likely to produce a representative sample?
questions.answer: Simple random sampling
questions.explanation: Simple random sampling gives every population member an equal chance of selection, minimizing systematic bias.

questions.question: The WEIRD problem highlights that most psychology research over-represents:
options: Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic samples
questions.answer: Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic samples
questions.explanation: The WEIRD critique argues that generalizing from such samples to all of humanity is problematic given their unrepresentativeness.

questions.question: Internal validity is best ensured by:
options: Random assignment and experimental control of confounds
Conducting the study in a natural setting
questions.answer: Random assignment and experimental control of confounds
questions.explanation: Internal validity — confidence that the IV caused the outcome — is maximized through random assignment and rigorous control of potential confounds.

questions.question: Content validity ensures that a test:
options: Adequately samples all aspects of the construct being measured
Correlates with other accepted tests of the same construct
questions.answer: Adequately samples all aspects of the construct being measured
questions.explanation: Content validity requires that test items representatively cover the full range of the construct being assessed.

questions.question: Statistical power can be increased by:
options: Using a two-tailed test instead of one-tailed
questions.answer: Increasing sample size
questions.explanation: Larger samples reduce standard error and increase the likelihood of detecting a true effect (increasing power).

questions.question: The Discussion section of an APA report does NOT typically include:
questions.answer: Raw data tables
questions.explanation: Raw data are presented in the Results section; the Discussion interprets findings, notes limitations, and proposes future directions.

questions.question: The goal of peer review is to:
options: Ensure research meets quality and ethical standards before publication
Guarantee that results are correct and will replicate
Allow the public to vote on what gets published
questions.answer: Ensure research meets quality and ethical standards before publication
questions.explanation: Peer review subjects manuscripts to independent expert evaluation, improving quality and catching errors before publication.

questions.question: A factorial ANOVA is used when a study has:
options: Two or more IVs and one DV
Repeated measurements on a single variable
questions.answer: Two or more IVs and one DV
questions.explanation: Factorial ANOVA examines the main effects of two or more independent variables and their interactions on a single dependent variable.

questions.question: Which is the most appropriate design to study changes in reading ability from ages 6 to 16?
questions.answer: Longitudinal study
questions.explanation: A longitudinal study follows the same individuals over time, directly tracking developmental changes in reading ability.

questions.question: A p-value represents:
options: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability of observing results at least as extreme as found, assuming the null is true
The effect size of the study
The probability that the alternative hypothesis is true
questions.answer: The probability of observing results at least as extreme as found, assuming the null is true
questions.explanation: The p-value is the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as those observed, calculated under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true.
